common_name: Red Turks Cap (Mexican Sleeping Hibiscus Shade-Tolerant Hummingbird Shrub)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; the Mexican Turk's Cap (var. mexicanus) grows larger and more vigorously than the native Texas Turk's Cap (var. drummondii) — allow adequate space, or prune back heavily each late winter to control size.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Red Turk's Cap (Malvaviscus arboreus var. mexicanus) is a Gulf South garden classic and one of the most important hummingbird-attracting plants for the region — a robust, fast-growing shrub producing bright red, tubular, never-fully-opening flowers from summer through frost, the twisted petals and protruding stamens creating the distinctive 'sleeping hibiscus' appearance that gives the plant one of its common names. The flower form — a narrow, twisted tube of red petals rather than the open cup of a blooming hibiscus — is precisely adapted to hummingbird feeding: only a long bill can access the nectar at the base, making Malvaviscus one of the premier hummingbird plants for the Gulf South. The Mexican variety grows larger, more vigorously, and with larger leaves and flowers than the native Texas Turk's Cap (var. drummondii), reaching 6–9 feet in a single growing season in warm zone 9 conditions. One of the exceptional qualities of Red Turk's Cap is its extraordinary shade tolerance — unlike most flowering shrubs, it blooms prolifically in partial to deep shade, filling the critical niche of shade-tolerant flowering shrub that few Gulf South plants can occupy. Under large live oaks and pines, where most flowering plants decline, Turk's Cap thrives and blooms from June through December. In zone 9, the tops may be killed by hard freezes but plants return vigorously from the roots in spring. Excellent for naturalizing in large shade garden areas.

common_name: Blue Plumbago (Sky Blue Everblooming Gulf South Shrub for Sunny Borders and Hedges)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Plumbago auriculata is one of the most widely used landscape shrubs in zone 9-11 Gulf South and Florida — it is frequently used as a large-scale mass planting or informal hedge where its fast growth and long bloom season create maximum visual impact.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Blue Plumbago is one of the most valued and widely planted flowering shrubs for Gulf South landscapes — an evergreen to semi-evergreen South African native that produces clusters of small, five-petaled, sky blue to pale periwinkle-blue flowers nearly continuously from spring through frost in zone 9, creating one of the most sustained flowering displays of any commonly grown landscape shrub. The sky blue flower color is genuinely unusual in the warm-season landscape palette and is particularly valuable as a cooling contrast to the warm orange, yellow, and red tropicals that dominate Gulf South summer gardens. The slightly sticky calyx is a minor nuisance (small pets and children can find seeds sticking to fur or clothing) but not a significant garden problem. Plumbago is fast-growing, drought-tolerant once established, salt-spray tolerant for coastal plantings, and adapts to a wide range of soil types including the poor sandy soils common along the Gulf Coast. In zone 9, the tops are often damaged or killed by hard freezes but plants return quickly from the roots in spring, producing new growth and beginning bloom by late spring. The fast regrowth after frost damage makes Plumbago one of the most resilient landscape shrubs for zone 8b–9 gardens. Cassius blue butterflies use Plumbago as their primary larval host plant — an important ecological function that makes Plumbago doubly valuable in Gulf South butterfly gardens.

common_name: Indigo Spires Salvia (Exceptionally Long Blue-Violet Spike Perennial Salvia Hybrid)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Indigo Spires grows to 4–5 feet and should be placed toward the back of mixed borders; the elongating flower spikes continue growing throughout the season, eventually reaching 12–18 inches in length by fall.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun to partial shade; Indigo Spires is a hybrid Salvia (S. farinacea x S. longispicata) producing one of the most spectacular and long-season blue-purple flower displays of any commonly grown perennial — tall, dense, deep blue-violet to indigo flower spikes that elongate continuously throughout the growing season, eventually reaching 12–18 inches in length by fall and creating a dramatic vertical blue accent that persists from late spring through hard frost in zone 9. The elongating spike behavior distinguishes Indigo Spires from most other salvias: rather than producing a flush of flowers and then declining or deadheading the spent portion, the spike continues adding new flowers at the growing tip while maintaining color lower down — a continuously expanding, self-renewing flower spike that becomes more impressive as the season progresses. The deep, rich indigo-blue color is more saturated and richer than standard Salvia farinacea cultivars — closer to a true indigo in the spectrum, creating a deeper, more intense blue effect in the summer border. Hummingbirds and swallowtail butterflies visit the flowers extensively. Indigo Spires grows vigorously to 4–5 feet and spreads by underground rhizomes — it naturalizes readily in Gulf South zone 9 gardens, returning from the roots each spring after frost kills the tops and expanding the planting gradually over years. Cut back to 12 inches in late winter to encourage fresh, vigorous regrowth.

common_name: Pink Powderpuff (Spectacular Tropical Small Tree with Giant Pink Pompom Flowers)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Pink Powderpuff is frost-sensitive and should be planted in the most frost-protected garden location in zone 9 — against south-facing walls, under building eaves, or in large containers that can be moved indoors during the brief winter freeze events that periodically affect zone 9.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Pink Powderpuff (Calliandra haematocephala) is one of the most visually spectacular flowering trees available for Gulf South zone 9b–12 gardens — a tropical South American legume tree producing large (3–4 inch diameter), perfect spherical pompoms composed entirely of long, bright pink to deep rose-red stamens, creating a flower display that looks like enormous powder puffs or fireworks suspended from the branches. The flower structure is unique: what appear to be the 'petals' are actually hundreds of long, silk-fine stamens arranged in a perfect sphere, each stamen tipped with a tiny bead of pollen — the combined effect of many stamens creating the full, lush, velvet-textured pompom that makes this plant immediately recognizable and unlike any other commonly grown flowering tree. The dramatic flowers appear in late winter through spring and often again in fall, hanging from branch tips in clusters that can cover the tree completely during peak bloom. In zone 9a, Pink Powderpuff is frost-sensitive and requires protection during hard freezes — many zone 9a gardeners grow it in large containers for easy winter protection, where it blooms abundantly. In zone 9b–12, it is a reliable, fast-growing evergreen tree. Hummingbirds visit the flowers for nectar; the long, silky stamens make it one of the most hummingbird-attractive trees in the tropical garden palette.

common_name: East Indian Lemon Grass (Tall Aromatic Culinary Lemon Grass for Tea and Cooking)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants after the last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; East Indian Lemon Grass is more cold-sensitive than West Indian Lemon Grass (C. citratus) and less commonly available in nurseries — propagate from seed or divisions from existing clumps; in zone 9, mulch heavily in winter to protect the crown from brief freezes.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; East Indian Lemon Grass (Cymbopogon flexuosus) is a tall, strongly aromatic grass from India and Sri Lanka — the primary source of lemongrass essential oil in commercial production worldwide and an excellent culinary and medicinal herb for Gulf South gardens where it grows as a warm-season perennial. The flavor and aroma are distinctly similar to but slightly different from West Indian Lemon Grass (C. citratus): both produce the characteristic bright, clean, citrus-lemon fragrance from the essential oil citral, but East Indian Lemon Grass is considered more intensely aromatic and is the preferred species for essential oil distillation. For culinary use, both species are suitable for Thai cooking, Vietnamese soups, herbal teas, and marinades — the inner stalks harvested and used the same way as standard lemongrass. East Indian Lemon Grass grows taller and more slenderly than C. citratus, reaching 4–6 feet with a more open, arching habit rather than the dense clumping habit of West Indian Lemon Grass — the differences in habit and aroma make it worth growing alongside the common species for comparison. In Gulf South zone 9, it grows as a warm-season perennial, potentially surviving mild winters with mulch protection but requiring replanting if hard freezes kill the crown. Fresh lemongrass harvested from the garden has a brighter, more vivid flavor than dried or frozen commercial lemongrass — a strong argument for growing your own in the Gulf South kitchen garden.
